elements.sent-2411-001: Traditional Learning-To-Rank (LETOR) approaches, including pairwise methods like RankNet and LambdaMART, often fall short by solely focusing on pairwise comparisons, leading to sub-optimal global rankings.
elements.sent-2411-002: Conversely, deep learning based listwise methods, while aiming to optimise entire lists, require complex tuning and yield only marginal improvements over robust pairwise models.
elements.sent-2411-003: To overcome these limitations, we introduce Travelling Salesman Problem Rank (TSPRank), a hybrid pairwise-listwise ranking method.
elements.sent-2411-004: TSPRank reframes the ranking problem as a Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP), a well-known combinatorial optimisation challenge that has been extensively studied for its numerous solution algorithms and applications.
elements.sent-2411-005: This approach enables the modelling of pairwise relationships and leverages combinatorial optimisation to determine the listwise ranking.
elements.sent-2411-006: This approach can be directly integrated as an additional component into embeddings generated by existing backbone models to enhance ranking performance.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our extensive experiments across three backbone models on diverse tasks, including stock ranking, information retrieval, and historical events ordering, demonstrate that TSPRank significantly outperforms both pure pairwise and listwise methods.
elements.sent-2411-008: Our qualitative analysis reveals that TSPRank's main advantage over existing methods is its ability to harness global information better while ranking.
elements.sent-2411-009: TSPRank's robustness and superior performance across different domains highlight its potential as a versatile and effective LETOR solution.

elements.sent-2411-011: Learning to Rank (LETOR) algorithms have become essential in applications such as recommendation systems \cite{ada-ranker,peiPersonalizedRerankingRecommendation2019,rank-insights , question answering \cite{DBLP:conf/aclnut/DengZL21,10.1145/2505515.2505670,campese-etal-2024-pre , and information retrieval \cite{aiLearningDeepListwise2018a,pang2020setrank,rankformer .}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: These algorithms aim to order a list of ranking entities based on their features, optimising for the most relevant or preferred entities to appear at the top.
elements.sent-2411-013: Recently, LETOR methods have also expanded into other domains, such as stock and portfolio selection \cite{SONG201720,portfolioselectionletor and textual ordering \cite{tan-etal-2013-learning,NEURIPS2023_f2a11632 .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Despite these broader applications of LETOR, latest fundamental work on LETOR still primarily focused on incorporating click data into ranking models and addressing biases introduced by user feedback \cite{10.1145/3569453,10.1145/3616855.3636451,10.1145/3580305.3599914,10.1145/3394486.3403336 , concentrating on retrieval and recommendation tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Limited research has explored general ranking methods across diverse tasks and domains.
elements.sent-2411-016: Over the past decade, research on LETOR models mainly focuses on pairwise and listwise approaches, while pointwise methods often fail to capture the intricate inter-entity relationships that are essential for accurate ranking.
elements.sent-2411-017: Pairwise methods, such as LambdaMART and RankNet \cite{lambdamart , primarily optimise for pairwise comparisons without a holistic view of the entire ranking list, potentially leading to sub-optimal global rankings.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Listwise methods optimise the ranking of entire lists directly rather than individuals or pairs.
elements.sent-2411-019: This category includes advanced neural network architectures, particularly adaptations of the Transformer architecture \cite{attention , such as Rankformer \cite{rankformer and SetRank \cite{pang2020setrank .}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: However, existing work shows that deep learning-based listwise models require complex tuning to achieve marginal gains over robust pairwise models like LambdaMART on information retrieval benchmarks \cite{google-neural-outperform-gbdt .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Therefore, pairwise and listwise methods each have inherent drawbacks and more effective solutions for LETOR have not been exhaustively explored.
elements.sent-2411-022: Recently, GNNRank successfully used directed graph neural networks to learn listwise rankings from pairwise comparisons \cite{he2022gnnrank .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Although their method is not applicable to general LETOR problems, as it requires pre-known pairwise relationships like the outcomes of sports matches, it still suggests a potential approach for LETOR by representing pairwise comparisons as a graph.
elements.sent-2411-024: Inspired by \citeauthor{abend-etal-2015-lexical , who used combinatorial optimisation techniques to recover the lexical order of recipe pieces \cite{abend-etal-2015-lexical , we propose Travelling Salesman Problem Rank (TSPRank).}}
elements.sent-2411-025: This hybrid pairwise-listwise method reframes the ranking problem as a Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP), an NP-hard combinatorial optimisation challenge that seeks the optimal sequence of node visits to minimise total travel cost.
elements.sent-2411-026: In a ranking context, TSPRank aims to find the optimal permutation of ranking entities to maximise the ranking score.
elements.sent-2411-027: Our main contribution, TSPRank, bridges the gap between pairwise and listwise ranking models through combinatorial optimisation, demonstrating strong robustness across diverse ranking problems.
elements.sent-2411-028: TSPRank simplifies the complex listwise ranking problem into easier pairwise comparisons, thus overcoming the challenges of directly learning listwise rankings in complex data and tasks.
elements.sent-2411-029: It also enhances pairwise methods by incorporating listwise optimisation through a TSP solver.
elements.sent-2411-030: To improve ranking performance, TSPRank can be applied directly as an additional component on embeddings generated from domain-specific backbone models.
elements.sent-2411-031: To our knowledge, it is the first LETOR model to frame the ranking problem as a combinatorial optimisation plus graph representation task.
elements.sent-2411-032: We demonstrate its superior performance across three backbone models on diverse ranking tasks: stock ranking, information retrieval, and historical events ordering, covering both numerical and textual data across multiple domains.
elements.sent-2411-033: We introduce two learning methods for TSPRank: a local method using a pre-defined ground truth adjacency matrix without the TSP solver during training and a global end-to-end method that includes the solver in the training loop for better model-inference alignment.
elements.sent-2411-034: Our empirical analysis visualises the predictions and graph connections to provide insights into TSPRank’s effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-035: We demonstrate that the TSP solver enhances the model’s ability to harness global information during ranking and increases tolerance to errors in pairwise comparisons.
elements.sent-2411-036: Additionally, we assess inference latency caused by the combinatorial optimisation solver and suggest potential solutions to mitigate this overhead.
elements.sent-2411-037: LETOR algorithms aim to rank entities based on their features.
elements.sent-2411-038: In LETOR, we denote a list of $N$ ranking entities as $\mathbf{e}$, containing $\{\mathbf{e _1, \mathbf{e _2, \dots, \mathbf{e _N\}$, with each $\mathbf{e _i \in \mathbb{R ^d$ where $d$ represents the dimensionality of the feature space.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-039: A scoring function $s: \mathbb{R ^{d \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is applied on the entities, which are then ranked in descending order of the $s(\mathbf{e _i)$ scores.}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: LETOR algorithms are broadly categorised into pointwise, pairwise, and listwise approaches based on their optimisation strategies.
elements.sent-2411-041: Pointwise methods treat ranking as a regression problem, comparing scores $s(\mathbf{e _i)$ to labels $y_i$.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Models such as OPRF~\cite{fuhr1989optimum , TreeBoost~\cite{friedman2001greedy , and RankSVM~\cite{shashua2002taxonomy , including newer implementations like RankCNN~\cite{severyn2015learning , are known for their computational efficiency but may not capture inter-entity relationships adequately.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Pairwise methods focus on the relative comparisons of entity pairs, assessing scores $(s(\mathbf{e _i), s(\mathbf{e _j))$ against binary labels $y_{ij}$, which denote if entity $\mathbf{e _i$ should be ranked higher than $\mathbf{e _j$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Methods in this category include RankNet~\cite{burges2005learning , LambdaRank~\cite{burges2006learning , and LambdaMART~\cite{lambdamart .}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: Listwise methods directly compute scores based on the features of all entities in a list.
elements.sent-2411-046: These methods aim to optimise a listwise objective, comparing the entire set of computed scores $s(\mathbf{e ) \in \mathbb{R ^N$ against a complete set of labels $\mathbf{y \in \mathbb{R ^N$.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-049: Considering that a list of entities can be viewed as a sequence, methodologies from Natural Language Processing (NLP) are applicable to ranking tasks.
elements.sent-2411-050: Notably, the Transformer architecture \cite{attention , has been adapted to address listwise ranking challenges, referred to as listwise Transformers in recent work \cite{peiPersonalizedRerankingRecommendation2019, pasumarthiPermutationEquivariantDocument2020, pang2020setrank, zhuangCrossPositionalAttentionDebiasing2021, liPEARPersonalizedReranking2022, rankformer .}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Although listwise ranking methods, primarily neural rankers, have demonstrated their advantages in web search reranking tasks \cite{aiLearningDeepListwise2018a, pang2020setrank , \citeauthor{google-neural-outperform-gbdt conducted a comprehensive evaluation comparing listwise neural rankers to the gradient-boosted-decision-tree (GBDT)-based pairwise LambdaMART \cite{lambdamart,google-neural-outperform-gbdt .}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: The results indicated that LambdaMART consistently outperforms listwise rankers across three widely used information retrieval datasets.
elements.sent-2411-053: Furthermore, it was observed that listwise rankers require substantial efforts to achieve only marginal improvements over LambdaMART.
elements.sent-2411-054: Consequently, it is evident that listwise ranking models are not universally applicable solutions and generally lack the robustness needed for broader deployment.

elements.sent-2411-056: Directly predicting the listwise order is challenging, as correctly ranking $N$ entities from 1 to $N$ is complex.
elements.sent-2411-057: However, breaking it down into $N \times N$ pairwise comparisons simplifies the task, as each pairwise comparison is more straightforward than the entire listwise ranking.
elements.sent-2411-058: However, a fundamental drawback of pairwise ranking, as highlighted by \citeauthor{pairwise-to-listwise \cite{pairwise-to-listwise , is that the learning objectives do not focus on minimising the listwise ranking errors.}}
elements.sent-2411-059: To address this gap and rethink the approach of pairwise ranking, we introduce TSPRank, a novel methodology for position-based ranking problems.
elements.sent-2411-060: This approach integrates listwise optimisation into pairwise comparisons by modelling ranking tasks as TSP.
elements.sent-2411-061: Notably, TSPRank can be applied as an additional component with embeddings generated from domain-specific backbone models to achieve improved ranking performance.
elements.sent-2411-062: We model the ranking problem as an open-loop Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP).
elements.sent-2411-063: Given a set of entities ${\mathbf{e _1, \mathbf{e _2, \ldots, \mathbf{e _N}$ and all the pairwise score values $s(\mathbf{e _i, \mathbf{e _j)$ indicating the gain of ranking entity $\mathbf{e _j$ immediately after $\mathbf{e _i$, we define a complete graph $G = (V, W)$, where $V$ represents the entities and $W$ represents the pairwise scores between every two entities.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Each entity $\mathbf{e _i$ in the ranking problem corresponds to a city in the TSP.}
elements.sent-2411-065: By mapping entities to cities and pairwise scores to weights, we can formulate the ranking problem as a TSP to find the optimal permutation $\pi$.
elements.sent-2411-066: Thus, the objective is to find an optimal open-loop tour $\pi^*$ of the entities defined in Equation~\ref{eq:op-prob such that the total pairwise score is maximised. \begin{equation}\label{eq:op-prob} \pi^* = \underset{\pi}{\mathrm{argmax}} \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} s(\mathbf{e}_{\pi(i)}, \mathbf{e}_{\pi(i+1)})\text{.}\end{equation} The next step is developing a scoring function that accurately models the pairwise relationships between the ranking entities, thereby effectively determining the scores.}
elements.sent-2411-067: The scoring model takes a set of ranking entities or their corresponding embeddings $\{\mathbf{e _1, \mathbf{e _2, \dots, \mathbf{e _N\}$, where each $\mathbf{e _i \in \mathbb{R ^d$ and $d$ is the dimension of the features or embeddings.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: It outputs an $N \times N$ weighted adjacency matrix $A$ that represents the pairwise scores of every two entities.
elements.sent-2411-069: The scoring model comprises an optional node encoder and a trainable bilinear model on top.
elements.sent-2411-070: The default encoder used is the Transformer encoder block \cite{attention , but it can be replaced with any other encoder suited to the specific task and dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-071: Additionally, using an encoder is optional if sufficiently robust backbone models are available.
elements.sent-2411-072: Given a pair of encoded representations of entities $(\mathbf{e _i, \mathbf{e _j)$, the bilinear model computes the pairwise score using the following bilinear form: \begin{equation}s(\mathbf{e}_i, \mathbf{e}_j) = \mathbf{e}_i^\intercal \mathbf{W} \mathbf{e}_j + b,\end{equation} where $\mathbf{W}$ and $b$ are learnable parameters that can be optimised along with the encoder parameters.}}
elements.sent-2411-073: The adjacency matrix $A$ is constructed as follows: \begin{equation}A_{ij} = s(\mathbf{e}_i, \mathbf{e}_j), \ \text{for all } i, j \in \{1, \dots, N\},\end{equation} where each entry $A_{ij}$ directly corresponds to the computed pairwise comparison score from entity $\mathbf{e _i$ to entity $\mathbf{e _j$.}}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}A_{ij} = s(e_i, e_j), \ for all  i, j \in \{1, \dots, N\},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-074: Inference of TSPRank, described in Equation~\ref{eq:op-prob , requires solving the open-loop TSP, which is widely recognised as an NP-hard combinatorial optimisation problem.}
elements.sent-2411-075: The solution algorithms of the TSP consist of exact algorithms (brunch and bound \cite{poikonen2019branch ) and approximation algorithms (ant colony optimisation \cite{yang2008ant ).}}
elements.sent-2411-076: Given the scale of the ranking problem and the desired ranking accuracy, we prefer to obtain an exact solution for the TSP rather than an approximate one.
elements.sent-2411-077: Therefore, we formulate the TSP as a Mixed Integer Linear Programming (MILP) problem \cite{rieck2010new , where the integer variables represent binary decisions on whether a path between two nodes is included in the optimal tour.}
elements.sent-2411-078: We then use an MILP optimization solver, such as CPLEX \cite{cplex or Gurobi \cite{gurobi , to find the exact optimal solution.}}
elements.sent-2411-079: The decision variables are defined as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:decision-var} x_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1, \text{if entity $\mathbf{e}_j$ is ranked immediately after $\mathbf{e}_i$.} 0, \text{otherwise.} \end{cases}\end{equation} We then apply the TSP optimisation problem defined in Appendix~\ref{appendix:tsp-constraints to ensure the result is a single, complete ranking that includes all entities.}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}\label{eq:decision-var} x_{ij} =  1, if entity $e_j$ is ranked immediately after $e_i$. 0, otherwise. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-080: This inference process effectively performs listwise optimisation, as it determines the optimal permutation of entities that maximises the overall pairwise ranking score.
elements.sent-2411-081: We introduce two distinct learning methodologies for the TSPRank model outlined in Section~\ref{sec:tsprank .}
elements.sent-2411-082: The first method is a local approach, focusing exclusively on individual pairwise comparisons during the training phase.
elements.sent-2411-083: The second method is a global, end-to-end approach, incorporating the Gurobi solver directly into the training process, thereby allowing the black-box MILP solver to influence the learning dynamically.

elements.sent-2411-149: For LambdaMART, we use 10,000 trees since previous experiments indicated that Rankformer failed to outperform LambdaMART with this configuration \cite{rankformer .}
elements.sent-2411-150: We choose the widely-used XGBoost\footnote{\url{https://xgboost.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tutorials/learning_to_rank.html \cite{xgboost implementation of LambdaMART, especially due to the performance enhancements in its version 2.0.}}}
elements.sent-2411-151: In the MQ2008-list dataset, a query might contain over 1,000 documents, resulting in a graph that is too large for the TSP solver to handle efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-152: Therefore, we focus on a reranking or post-reranking stage.
elements.sent-2411-153: Specifically, we extract the top 10 and top 30 documents for each query to conduct two separate experiments.
elements.sent-2411-154: This approach allows us to manage the computational complexity while still evaluating the effectiveness of TSPRank in a realistic ranking scenario.
elements.sent-2411-155: We use similar hyperparameters as in the stock ranking task but increase the number of transformer layers in both Rankformer and TSPRank to four, as we do not use any pretrained embeddings here.
elements.sent-2411-156: To ensure fairness, we also follow the 5-fold cross-validation setup as provided by Microsoft.
elements.sent-2411-157: We randomly allocate the events into ranking groups and train the ranking models to predict the ground truth order of occurrences within these groups.
elements.sent-2411-158: We test with group sizes of 10, 30 and 50 for fair comparisons.
elements.sent-2411-159: For each group size, we generate the group allocations using five different random seeds and report the average performance to mitigate the effects of random group allocation.
elements.sent-2411-160: Regarding the models, we observed overfitting with Rankformer and TSPRank when including the transformer block, even with a 0.5 dropout rate.
elements.sent-2411-161: This is likely due to the strong representational power of the contextual embedding and the relatively small size of the OTD2 dataset compared to other NLP corpora.
elements.sent-2411-162: Consequently, we retained only the scoring network in Rankformer and the trainable bilinear model in TSPRank, integrating them directly with the text-embedding-3-small embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-163: This setting not only prevents overfitting but also ensures similar model sizes.
elements.sent-2411-164: We remove the weights in the weighted loss function for TSPRank-Local here since all events should be weighted equally without any additional requirements in this task.
elements.sent-2411-165: Given that our task encompasses both ranking and stock selection functionalities, we employ both traditional ranking metrics and financial performance metrics in our evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-166: For ranking effectiveness, we use the Mean Average Precision at $K$ (MAP@K), which evaluates the precision of the top $K$ ranked stocks by averaging the precision scores at each relevant stock position.
elements.sent-2411-167: We also use Kendall's Tau, defined as: $\tau = \frac{(C - D) {\frac{1 {2 n (n-1)}$, where \( C \) is the number of concordant pairs (pairs of entities that are in the same order in both predicted and ground truth rankings), \( D \) is the number of discordant pairs (pairs of entities that are in different orders in the rankings), and \( n \) is the total number of entities being ranked.}}}
elements.sent-2411-168: Kendall's Tau is a correlation coefficient that measures the ordinal association between two ranked lists.
elements.sent-2411-169: This is suitable in our case, where we need to assess the similarity between the predicted rankings and the ground-truth rankings.
elements.sent-2411-170: Furthermore, to assess the financial impact of the rankings, we simulate trading activities by predicting the rankings of all individual stocks in every sector for the next trading day $t+1$ on trading day $t$.
elements.sent-2411-171: This simulation is consistent with the one presented by \citeauthor{temporal-stock-prediction \cite{temporal-stock-prediction .}}
elements.sent-2411-172: We then rank these stocks, hold the top $k$ stocks $S_k$, and sell them on trading day $t+1$.
elements.sent-2411-173: The price of stock $i$ on day $t$ is denoted as $p_i^{(t)}$ per share.
elements.sent-2411-174: The cumulative Investment Return Ratio (IRR) and Sharpe Ratio (SR) are defined in Equation~\ref{eq:irr-and-sr , where $R_p$ is the return of the portfolio, $R_f$ is the risk-free rate, and $\sigma$ is the standard deviation: \begin{equation}\label{eq:irr-and-sr} \text{IRR}^k = \sum_{i \in S^k} \frac{p_i^{(t)} - p_i^{(t-1)}}{p_i^{(t-1)}}, \text{ SR} = \frac{E(R_p-R_f)}{\sigma(R_p)}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-175: We evaluate the performance of our models using several well-established ranking metrics: Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG) at various cutoff levels (3, 5, 10), Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR), and Kendall's tau.
elements.sent-2411-176: NDCG measures a document's usefulness based on its position in the result list, with higher positions receiving more weight.
elements.sent-2411-177: On the other hand, MRR assesses the rank position of the first relevant document, calculated as the average of the reciprocal ranks of the first relevant document for each query.
elements.sent-2411-178: The formula for MRR is defined by $\text{MRR = \frac{1 {|Q| \sum_{i=1 ^{|Q| \frac{1 {\text{rank _i}$, where $|Q|$ is the total number of queries and $\text{rank _i$ is the rank position of the first relevant document for the $i$-th query.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-179: We report the Kendall's Tau, MRR, Exact Match (EM) of ordering, and the Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) of the predicted and ground truth rankings.
elements.sent-2411-180: EM is defined as the ratio of the number of events whose predicted order exactly matches the ground truth order to the total number of events, formulated as: $\text{EM = \frac{\text{\# correctly ordered events {\text{\# events }$.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-187: The results highlight the comparisons among three types of models: pairwise (LambdaMART), listwise (Rankformer), and our pairwise-listwise method (TSPRank).
elements.sent-2411-188: Better Performance of Pairwise-Listwise Method Across Diverse Tasks (RQ1).
elements.sent-2411-189: Our pairwise-listwise method, TSPRank-Global, demonstrates outstanding robustness and superior performance across diverse datasets and domains.
elements.sent-2411-190: For instance, on the NASDAQ stock ranking dataset (Table~\ref{tab:stock-results ), TSPRank-Global achieves a Kendall's Tau of 0.0447, significantly surpassing both LambdaMART (0.0071) and Rankformer (0.0110).}
elements.sent-2411-191: Similarly, on the NYSE dataset (Table~\ref{tab:stock-results ), TSPRank-Global attains a Kendall's Tau of 0.0422, outperforming LambdaMART (0.0054) and Rankformer (0.0181).}
elements.sent-2411-192: Both TSPRank-Local and TSPRank-Global also consistently achieve higher IRR and SR, yielding improved financial performance.
elements.sent-2411-193: The model's robustness is further evidenced by its consistent top performance across other datasets.
elements.sent-2411-194: For example, in the MQ2008-list (Table~\ref{tab:mq2008-results ), TSPRank-Global leads with NDCG@10 scores of 0.8884 for the top 10 documents and 0.7631 for the top 30 documents, indicating superior ranking accuracy, despite a minor difference in Kendall's Tau compared to Rankformer (around 0.01 to 0.02).}
elements.sent-2411-195: Additionally, TSPRank-Global achieves top performance in the historical events ordering task (Table~\ref{tab:historical-events-results ), regardless of the group size (10, 30, and 50).}
elements.sent-2411-196: TSPRank Can Be Extra Component Upon Embeddings For Boosting The Performance.
elements.sent-2411-197: In the stock ranking and ordering tasks for historical events, we directly deployed TSPRank on the stock embeddings and text embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-198: As demonstrated, TSPRank effectively serves as an additional component on embeddings, significantly enhancing ranking performance.
elements.sent-2411-199: This capability allows TSPRank to have broader applications across various domains.
elements.sent-2411-200: Stronger Robustness Towards Number of entities.
elements.sent-2411-201: TSPRank-Global also shows greater robustness concerning the number of entities compared to the pure pairwise LambdaMART and listwise Rankformer.
elements.sent-2411-202: As evidenced by the MQ2008 (Table~\ref{tab:mq2008-results ) results, the gap between LambdaMART and Rankformer narrows as the number of documents increases from 10 to 30, with the NDCG@3 score difference between these two methods reducing from -0.0387 to -0.0146.}
elements.sent-2411-203: Similarly, in historical events ordering (Table~\ref{tab:historical-events-results ), LambdaMART and Rankformer's performance fluctuates more significantly with group size changes.}
elements.sent-2411-204: This is evident from the varying differences in their Kendall's Tau and MRR compared to those of TSPRank-Global.
elements.sent-2411-205: Conversely, TSPRank-Global consistently performs well regardless of group size, maintaining top performance across most metrics.
elements.sent-2411-207: As evidenced by the performance of TSPRank-Global compared to TSPRank-Local on MQ2008 and OTD2.
elements.sent-2411-208: For example, on the OTD2 dataset (Table~\ref{tab:historical-events-results ), TSPRank-Global achieves a Kendall's Tau of 0.6301, 0.6302, 0.6207 for the three different group sizes, compared to TSPRank-Local’s 0.5658, 0.5095, and 0.4713.}
elements.sent-2411-209: These results suggest that the end-to-end global optimisation approach of TSPRank-Global aligns more effectively with overall optimisation goals, whereas the non-end-to-end method may not perfectly align with global optimisation by the TSP solver.
elements.sent-2411-210: LambdaMART Does Not Always Outperform Deep Learning Based Ranking Algorithm (RQ3).
elements.sent-2411-211: Although \citeauthor{google-neural-outperform-gbdt suggests that GBDT models, such as LambdaMART, generally outperform deep learning models on standard information retrieval datasets \cite{google-neural-outperform-gbdt , our results indicate that this is not always the case in other contexts.}}
elements.sent-2411-212: As demonstrated in Table~\ref{tab:stock-results , within the domain-specific scenario of stock ranking, LambdaMART shows markedly poorer performance.}
elements.sent-2411-213: Specifically, LambdaMART achieves a Kendall's Tau of 0.0071 on NASDAQ and 0.0054 on NYSE, trailing behind Rankformer (0.0110 and 0.0181) and TSPRank-Global (0.0447 and 0.0422).
elements.sent-2411-215: In both the top 10 and top 30 document settings, LambdaMART consistently underperforms Rankformer and TSPRank-Global when adapting to ordinal ranking labels across all metrics.
elements.sent-2411-216: These results underscore that while LambdaMART performs well with binary relevance labels or relevance levels labels, it is not always the optimal choice for recovering complete rankings.
elements.sent-2411-217: Overall, TSPRank's success suggests that combining pairwise and listwise approaches helps the model capture the relative ordering of entities and the overall ranking structure.
elements.sent-2411-218: This dual focus likely contributes to its superior performance across various datasets and metrics, making it versatile and powerful.
elements.sent-2411-219: As mentioned before, our pairwise-listwise TSPRank addresses both the lack of listwise optimisation in pairwise LambdaMART and the robustness issues in listwise Rankformer, which arise from attempting to predict the complete rankings directly.
elements.sent-2411-220: To empirically validate this and answer RQ4 (the advantages of hybrid pairwise-listwise TSPRank over other methods), we conducted another experiment on the OTD2 dataset, chosen because of the better interpretability provided by text.
elements.sent-2411-221: We arbitrarily sample three events each from the US, UK, and China, ensuring that the events within each country occurred at least 50 years apart (detailed in Table~\ref{tab:manually-events-data ).}
elements.sent-2411-222: We then use the pretrained LambdaMART, Rankformer, and TSPRank-Global from the experiments described in Section~\ref{sec:exp-setup to predict the rankings for this constructed group.}
elements.sent-2411-223: We visualise the predictions given by the three rankers in Figure~\ref{fig:prediction-visualisation .}
elements.sent-2411-224: Both the pairwise LambdaMART and pairwise-listwise TSPRank-Global successfully recover the intra-country event order, correctly identifying the relative order within the same colour group.
elements.sent-2411-225: However, the listwise Rankformer incorrectly predicts that ``US-2'' occurs earlier than ``US-1''.
elements.sent-2411-226: Although LambdaMART successfully recovers the intra-country event order, it makes mistakes in the overall ranking, particularly failing to recover the correct order of [``US-1'', ``UK-1'', ``CN-1''] and [``US-3'', ``UK-3'', ``CN-3''], validating its weaker ability for listwise ranking optimisation.
elements.sent-2411-227: In contrast, TSPRank accurately recovers most orders, with only a minor error between ``US-2'' and ``UK-2'', which only have a 30-year gap (1934 and 1904).
elements.sent-2411-228: We also visualise the intra-country connections using the adjacency matrix predicted by TSPRank's bilinear model in Figure~\ref{fig:tsprank-visualisation , omitting cross-country connections for clarity.}
elements.sent-2411-229: As highlighted in red, the direct comparison between ``US-1'' and ``US-3'' is incorrect since $s(\text{US-1 , \text{US-3 )$ should be higher than $s(\text{US-3 , \text{US-1 )$, but the model fails in this case.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-230: However, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:prediction-visualisation , TSPRank still correctly places ``US-1'' at position 3 and ``US-3'' at position 8.}
elements.sent-2411-231: This indicates that the model benefits from the cross-country connections and the listwise optimisation provided by the TSP solver.
elements.sent-2411-232: This makes the model more tolerant to errors or uncertainties in pairwise comparisons, as the listwise optimisation ensures that the highest-scoring permutation is selected.

elements.sent-2411-234: Incorporating a combinatorial optimisation solver is known to cause additional computational overhead, which can impact the inference latency.
elements.sent-2411-235: Therefore, we compare the average inference time per ranking group for TSPRank and Rankformer on the OTD2 historical events ordering dataset.
elements.sent-2411-236: The experimental setup and detailed results are provided in Appendix~\ref{appendix:latency .}
elements.sent-2411-237: We observe that the inference time for TSPRank increases exponentially with the number of ranking entities, primarily due to the discrete TSP solver.
elements.sent-2411-238: When the group size reaches 100, the solver consumes approximately 99.8\% of the inference time, while the encoding time remains similar to that of Rankformer.
elements.sent-2411-239: Therefore, TSPRank is more suitable for small-scale ranking problems with fewer than 30 entities, where its inference time is comparable to Rankformer.
elements.sent-2411-240: It is also advantageous in scenarios where TSPRank’s performance improvements outweigh the increased latency.
elements.sent-2411-241: Solvers like Gurobi are efficient for small TSP graphs.
elements.sent-2411-242: However, exact solutions for larger graphs require specialised techniques, such as the Concorde TSP Solver\footnote{\url{https://www.math.uwaterloo.ca/tsp/concorde/index.html , which uses cutting-plane algorithms and a branch-and-bound approach to solve graphs with up to 85,900 nodes \cite{applegate2006traveling .}}}
elements.sent-2411-243: Beyond exact solvers, heuristic algorithms like the Christofides–Serdyukov algorithm \cite{christofides2022worst and the Lin–Kernighan–Helsgaun heuristic \cite{lin1973effective,helsgaun2000effective,helsgaun2017extension are also used.}}
elements.sent-2411-244: Moreover, recent advances in neural networks have enabled the use of deep learning to approximate the TSP reward function, employing reinforcement learning to solve the problem \cite{bello2016neural,khalil2017learning,kool2018attention .}
elements.sent-2411-245: While we do not delve deeply into the various TSP solutions in this paper, we demonstrate that inference latency stems from the TSP solver and discuss potential enhancements for reducing inference latency and tackling large-scale ranking problems.
elements.sent-2411-246: This study introduces TSPRank, a novel pairwise-listwise approach for position-based ranking tasks, by modelling them as the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP).
elements.sent-2411-247: We present two learning methods for TSPRank, integrating listwise optimization into pairwise comparisons to address the limitations of traditional pure pairwise and listwise ranking models.
elements.sent-2411-248: TSPRank's application to diverse backbone models and datasets, including stock ranking, information retrieval, and historical events ordering, demonstrates its superior performance and robustness.
elements.sent-2411-249: Key findings include TSPRank's ability to outperform existing models such as LambdaMART and Rankformer across multiple datasets and various metrics.
elements.sent-2411-250: Furthermore, TSPRank's capability to function as an additional component on embeddings suggests its versatility and potential for broader applications across different domains.
elements.sent-2411-251: Our empirical analysis reveals that TSPRank’s superior performance is due to the discrete TSP solver’s ability to more effectively utilise listwise information.
elements.sent-2411-252: It also enhances TSPRank’s tolerance to uncertainties in pairwise comparisons by ensuring the selection of the highest-scoring permutation.
elements.sent-2411-253: Future work could explore the use of alternative TSP solvers and further enhancements to the Gurobi solver to reduce inference latency and improve scalability.. \bibliographystyle{ACM-Reference-Format .}

elements.sent-2411-269: We provide the inference times for various group sizes: 5, 10, 30, 50, and 100.
elements.sent-2411-270: The comparison shows that the inference time for TSPRank increases exponentially with the number of ranking entities in a group.
elements.sent-2411-271: This increase is primarily due to the discrete TSP solver, as indicated by the overlapping blue and red lines.

elements.sent-2411-273: When the group size reaches 100, approximately 99.8\% of the inference time is consumed by the solver.
elements.sent-2411-274: The encoding time from the input to the adjacency matrix remains similar to that of Rankformer (purple).

elements.sent-2411-278: TSPRank can be directly integrated as an additional component into embeddings generated by existing backbone models to enhance ranking performance.
elements.sent-2411-279: Learning to Rank (LETOR) algorithms have become essential in applications such as recommendation systems \cite{ada-ranker,peiPersonalizedRerankingRecommendation2019,rank-insights , question answering \cite{10.1145/2505515.2505670,campese-etal-2024-pre , and information retrieval \cite{aiLearningDeepListwise2018a,pang2020setrank,rankformer .}}}
elements.sent-2411-280: Despite these broader applications of LETOR, latest fundamental work on LETOR still primarily focused on incorporating click data into ranking models and addressing biases introduced by user feedback \cite{10.1145/3569453,10.1145/3580305.3599914,10.1145/3394486.3403336 , concentrating on retrieval and recommendation tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-281: The scoring model takes a set of ranking entities or their corresponding embeddings $\{\mathbf{e _1, \mathbf{e _2, \dots, \mathbf{e _N\}$ as input, where each $\mathbf{e _i \in \mathbb{R ^d$ and $d$ is the dimension of the features or embeddings.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-282: To ensure the desired ranking accuracy, we prefer to obtain an exact solution for the TSP rather than an approximate one.
elements.sent-2411-283: Therefore, we formulate the TSP as a Mixed Integer Linear Programming (MILP) problem \cite{rieck2010new , where the integer variables represent binary decisions on whether an edge between two entities is included in the optimal tour.}
elements.sent-2411-284: We then use an MILP optimisation solver, such as CPLEX \cite{cplex or Gurobi \cite{gurobi , to find the exact optimal solution.}}
elements.sent-2411-285: The decision variables are defined as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:decision-var} x_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1, \text{if entity $\mathbf{e}_j$ is ranked immediately after $\mathbf{e}_i$,} 0, \text{otherwise.} \end{cases}\end{equation} We then apply the TSP optimisation problem defined in Appendix~\ref{appendix:tsp-constraints to ensure the result is a single, complete ranking that includes all entities.}
elements.formula-2411-010: \begin{equation}\label{eq:decision-var} x_{ij} =  1, if entity $e_j$ is ranked immediately after $e_i$, 0, otherwise. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-286: The second method is a global, end-to-end approach, incorporating the Gurobi solver directly into the training process, thereby allowing the black-box MILP solver to influence the learning dynamically \cite{li-etal-2023-bert .}
elements.sent-2411-287: In the local learning approach, the objective is to understand pairwise consecutive relationships between entities.

elements.sent-2411-289: The structured score $\Delta (\mathbf{x , \mathbf{x ^t)$, defined in Equation~\ref{eq:structured-score , aims to enforce a margin for each incorrectly identified edge: \begin{equation}\label{eq:structured-score} \Delta (\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}^t) = \sum_{i=1}^N \sum_{j=1}^N \max (0, x_{ij} - x_{ij}^t)\text{.}\end{equation} This approach eliminates the need to manually define the target adjacency matrix.}}}
elements.sent-2411-290: Instead, it uses the output from the black-box optimiser to directly guide the updates of the model parameters.
elements.sent-2411-291: The task of stock ranking focuses on accurately predicting and ranking stocks according to their anticipated future returns ratio, which aids investors in selecting stocks for investment purposes~\citep{temporal-stock-prediction, zhang2022constructing .}
elements.sent-2411-292: Given that our task encompasses both ranking and stock selection functionalities, we use both traditional ranking metrics and financial performance metrics in our evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-293: Beyond exact solvers, heuristic algorithms like the Christofides–Serdyukov algorithm \cite{christofides2022worst and the Lin–Kernighan–Helsgaun heuristic \cite{lin1973effective,helsgaun2000effective are also used.}}
elements.sent-2411-294: Moreover, recent advances in neural networks have enabled the use of deep learning to approximate the TSP reward function, using reinforcement learning to solve the problem \cite{bello2016neural,khalil2017learning,kool2018attention .}
elements.sent-2411-295: Future research could further integrate these approaches to balance efficiency and accuracy in solving large-scale ranking tasks.
elements.sent-2411-296: This paper introduces TSPRank, a novel pairwise-listwise approach for position-based ranking tasks, by modelling them as the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP).
elements.sent-2411-297: We present two learning methods for TSPRank, integrating listwise optimisation into pairwise comparisons to address the limitations of traditional pure pairwise and listwise ranking models.
elements.sent-2411-298: Our empirical analysis reveals that TSPRank’s superior performance is due to the discrete TSP solver’s ability to more effectively use listwise information.
elements.sent-2411-299: Future work could explore the use of alternative TSP solvers and further enhancements to the Gurobi solver to reduce inference latency and improve scalability.
